claim: Two divergent agent types are emerging: long-horizon agents (minutes/hours, code execution, planning, subagents) for knowledge work, and low-latency customer experience agents (voice, sales, support) requiring different tech.
actionable: Design your agent for one use case: long-horizon needs orchestration and planning skills; customer-facing needs low latency and brand consistency via voice.
source_title: The Future of AI Agents: What Will Interrupt 2027 Look Like? | Interrupt 26

claim: Native speech-to-speech models (e.g., OpenAI V2) are emerging but not yet steerable enough for production; the voice-to-text-to-voice pipeline remains the standard for controllable agents.
actionable: For voice agents, use text-based intermediaries (speech→text→LLM→text→speech) until native speech models become more steerable; avoid native speech models for high-control applications now.
source_title: The Future of AI Agents: What Will Interrupt 2027 Look Like? | Interrupt 26

claim: All agentic systems need sandboxes (isolated execution environments) to enable safe code execution for data analysis, web browsing, image generation, and deep research—not just software development.
actionable: Grant agents sandbox access for non-coding tasks: marketing teams get AI tools to write scripts, engineers get code execution, analysts get data manipulation in isolated environments.
source_title: The Future of AI Agents: What Will Interrupt 2027 Look Like? | Interrupt 26

claim: Open-source models are approaching frontier model performance (per benchmarks) and offer cost advantages and domain fine-tuning options, particularly for coding agents that burn tokens fast.
actionable: Benchmark open-source models (Qwen, Llama, Gemma) on your specific tasks; invest in fine-tuning with your own traces to achieve frontier quality at 1/5 the cost.
source_title: The Future of AI Agents: What Will Interrupt 2027 Look Like? | Interrupt 26

claim: Agent identity patterns split into per-user auth (agent uses user's credentials, sees user-specific data) and fixed service accounts (agent has fixed credentials, same response for all users)—both are viable.
actionable: Choose per-user auth for personalized agents (each user gets different results based on their data); use service accounts for shared agents (consistent behavior, lower friction).
source_title: The Future of AI Agents: What Will Interrupt 2027 Look Like? | Interrupt 26

claim: Continual learning of agentic systems happens at three layers: model (fine-tuning on domain data), harness (editing system prompts/tool selections), and context (skills, agent.md, examples)—all improvable independently.
actionable: Separate learning loops: fine-tune the model on failures, refactor the harness code, add skills to the context layer. Track which layer drives improvement.
source_title: The Future of AI Agents: What Will Interrupt 2027 Look Like? | Interrupt 26

claim: Evals act as gradient signals for agent improvement: run agents on test sets, collect feedback, pass results to agentic systems that edit the harness. This is like gradient descent for non-differentiable systems.
actionable: Build evaluation suites as the forcing function for improvement; use agent-editable harnesses that automatically iterate on eval failures.
source_title: The Future of AI Agents: What Will Interrupt 2027 Look Like? | Interrupt 26

claim: Future agent builders won't be just engineers—domain experts (marketers, support teams, product) will build agents directly, not just provide feedback. This is the real unlock for agent adoption.
actionable: Design no-code agent builders (like LangSmith Fleet) that let domain experts define their own agents with natural language prompts and skills, no coding required.
source_title: The Future of AI Agents: What Will Interrupt 2027 Look Like? | Interrupt 26

claim: LangSmith Fleet agents built by non-engineers showed 240% improvement in lead-to-qualified-conversion and saved 40 hours/month per sales rep, proving domain-expert-led agent building is production-viable.
actionable: Empower non-technical domain experts to build their own agents with no-code platforms; measure ROI per use case to justify agent investment.
source_title: The Future of AI Agents: What Will Interrupt 2027 Look Like? | Interrupt 26
